run.output: Query executed successfully. Output: Query returned 4 rows, Execution time: 77.61-81.81 ms

validation.comparison_result: Results match ground truth: 4 rows, all aggregate values correct to 6 decimal places

profiling.notes: perf not available; estimated analysis based on code inspection and hardware characteristics

Q1.notes: Consistent execution time 77-81 ms across runs. 4 output groups (A,F), (N,F), (N,O), (R,F) with correct aggregates.

Q1.bottleneck: Multi-threaded aggregation with lock contention at merge phase; possible false sharing on hash table updates
Q1.root_cause: Despite 64 logical cores and morsel-driven parallelism, global mutex serializes the final merge of local aggregation maps. 59.99M rows scanned across 64 threads (morsel_size=100K) produces 59M row reads but only 4 groups, so contention at lock during merge dominates relative to scan time.

analysis.overall: Q1 is executing correctly and delivering solid performance (79.2 ms). However, the query has room for optimization on a 64-core system with HDD storage. Comparison with DuckDB (86.4-152.6 ms on same data) shows GenDB is 1.1-1.9x faster, but room exists for further improvement. Key bottlenecks: (1) lock contention during local→global aggregation merge, (2) suboptimal hash table sizing/type (std::unordered_map is slower than compact hash tables), (3) no SIMD vectorization of the filter predicate, (4) madvise hints could benefit from zone map-based prefetching, (5) false sharing on hash table bucket accesses across threads.

performance_optimizations.technique: Compact Hash Table (e.g., Swiss Table or Abseil FlatHashMap)
performance_optimizations.description: Replace std::unordered_map with a compact hash table (Abseil's FlatHashMap or a custom open-addressing implementation like Swiss Tables). std::unordered_map uses chaining with separate allocations, causing cache misses and slower lookups. For 6 result groups, a compact table sized to ~8-16 entries (75% load factor on power-of-2 sizing) will have near-perfect cache behavior. Expected improvement: 15-25% on the hot aggregation loop due to eliminated pointer chasing and better cache locality during merge phase.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-5 ms (4-6% gain) from reduced hash table lookup latency in local aggregation loops and merge

performance_optimizations.description: Vectorize the l_shipdate filter predicate using AVX2 to process 8 int32_t values per instruction (AVX2 256-bit register / 32 bits per value). The filter `l_shipdate[i] <= 10471` currently runs as a scalar comparison per row in the inner loop (line 157). With AVX2 vectorization, process 8 comparisons in parallel, producing a bitmask of matching rows. This requires: (1) loop over chunks of 8 rows, (2) load 8 dates via aligned _mm256_load, (3) compare via _mm256_cmple_epi32, (4) extract bitmask, (5) process matching indices. Benefit on 59.99M rows: 8x reduction in comparison operations. Expected improvement: 5-10% overall (4-8 ms) from reduced filter CPU.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 4-8 ms (5-10% gain) from vectorized filtering on 59.99M rows

performance_optimizations.technique: Zone Map-Based Prefetching and Block Skipping
performance_optimizations.description: The storage design includes zone maps (lineitem.l_shipdate.zmap) with min/max bounds per 100K-row block. Currently, the query loads all 59.99M rows sequentially without block skipping. Implement: (1) read zone map metadata at startup, (2) before scanning each 100K-row morsel, check if zone map indicates the entire block is outside the filter range (min > 10471), (3) if true, skip loading that block's columns entirely. For Q1 with 94% selectivity (0.94), expect ~6% of blocks to be fully skippable. Additionally, prefetch the next morsel's l_shipdate data using madvise(MADV_WILLNEED) or explicit prefetch to hide I/O latency. Expected improvement: 3-6 ms (4-8% gain) from reduced I/O.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-6 ms (4-8% gain) from block skipping and prefetching on ~6% of blocks

performance_optimizations.description: The current merge phase (lines 190-203) uses a global mutex to serialize updates to global_aggregation. With 64 threads contending on a single lock, this becomes a bottleneck. Replace with: (1) atomic CAS loop for updating shared aggregation state, or (2) per-socket/per-NUMA-node aggregation with cascade merge (two-level hierarchy), or (3) batch local results before merging to reduce lock frequency. For Q1 with only 6 output groups, option 1 (atomic CAS on group summaries) is simplest: use std::atomic<long double> for each aggregate field per group, update via compare-and-swap loops. Risk: floating-point atomics not directly supported on all platforms; may require thread_local accumulators with careful ordering. Expected improvement: 2-4 ms (2-5% gain) from reduced lock contention.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-4 ms (2-5% gain) from reduced lock contention on final merge

performance_optimizations.description: The scan-filter-aggregate loop (lines 155-186) processes one row at a time with several dependent operations (filter check, dictionary decode, arithmetic). Fuse by: (1) unroll the inner loop to process 4-8 rows per iteration, reducing loop overhead, (2) interleave computations to expose instruction-level parallelism (ILP), (3) batch dictionary lookups if possible. GCC -O3 may auto-vectorize and unroll, but explicit direction (e.g., `#pragma omp simd collapse(1)`) can help. Expected improvement: 2-3% from reduced loop overhead and increased ILP.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1-2 ms (1-2% gain) from reduced loop overhead and ILP

performance_optimizations.description: Q1's aggregation has only 6 output groups, which is tiny. This is a degenerate case where a compact array (indexed by [returnflag][linestatus] = 3 * 2 = 6 entries) would be faster than hash tables. Implement runtime cardinality detection: (1) sample first 10K rows to estimate output groups, (2) if <= 32 groups, use a direct array lookup (AggregationResult[returnflag_idx][linestatus_idx]), (3) else fall back to hash table. For Q1, this switches to O(1) array indexing, eliminating hash function computation and collision resolution entirely. Expected improvement: 5-8 ms (6-10% gain) from eliminated hash operations.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-8 ms (6-10% gain) from O(1) perfect hashing via direct array indexing

summary: Q1 (Iteration 0) passes all tests with correct results and solid absolute performance (79.2 ms). Compared to DuckDB (86.4 ms best), GenDB is 1.09x faster. However, relative to the hardware capacity (64 cores, 376GB RAM, HDD), several optimization opportunities exist. Top priority: (1) Replace std::unordered_map with a compact hash table like Abseil's FlatHashMap (4-6% gain, low risk). (2) Implement SIMD vectorized filtering for l_shipdate (5-10% gain, medium risk). (3) Exploit zone maps for block-level skipping (4-8% gain, medium risk). (4) Perfect-hash the 6 output groups via direct array indexing (6-10% gain, low risk). Combined, these optimizations could yield 15-25% total speedup (60-64 ms) without major structural changes. Recommended next step: implement option 6 (array-based aggregation) first due to low risk and high gain, then option 1 (compact hash tables) if further improvement is needed.
